title: Overfitting
definition: When a model learns the training data too well — including noise and irrelevant patterns — resulting in excellent training performance but poor generalization to new, unseen data. High variance, low bias.
why_used: Understanding overfitting is fundamental — it is the primary failure mode of ML models and the core reason why train/test split and cross-validation exist.
interview_point: Signs of overfitting: large gap between training accuracy (high) and validation accuracy (lower). Causes: model too complex, too few training samples, no regularization. Solutions: add regularization (L1/L2, Dropout), reduce model complexity, early stopping, data augmentation, more training data.
limitations: Fails on any data outside the training distribution
use_cases: What to avoid in any ML system
real_world_example:
```python
from sklearn.tree import DecisionTreeClassifier
from sklearn.model_selection import cross_val_score

# Overfit: fully grown tree
overfit_model = DecisionTreeClassifier(max_depth=None)  # Unlimited depth
overfit_model.fit(X_train, y_train)
print(f'Train score: {overfit_model.score(X_train, y_train):.4f}')  # ~1.0
print(f'Test score:  {overfit_model.score(X_test, y_test):.4f}')    # Much lower!

# Solutions:
# 1. Regularization (limit depth)
good_model = DecisionTreeClassifier(max_depth=5, min_samples_leaf=10)

# 2. Ensemble (Random Forest reduces variance)
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestClassifier
rf = RandomForestClassifier(n_estimators=200)

# 3. Cross-validation to detect overfitting early
scores = cross_val_score(overfit_model, X, y, cv=5)
print(f'CV scores: {scores.mean():.4f} ± {scores.std():.4f}')
```

title: Underfitting
definition: When a model is too simple to capture the underlying patterns in the data. Results in poor performance on BOTH training and test data. High bias, low variance.
why_used: Understanding the bias-variance tradeoff. Underfitting is the opposite failure mode to overfitting — the model lacks capacity to learn.
interview_point: Signs of underfitting: poor training accuracy (not just test accuracy). Causes: model too simple, too many regularization constraints, too few features, too little training time. Solutions: increase model complexity (more layers, more estimators), reduce regularization, add more features, train longer.
limitations: Cannot capture patterns even in training data
use_cases: What to avoid — signal to increase model complexity
real_world_example:
```python
# Underfit: linear model on non-linear data
from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression
from sklearn.datasets import make_circles

X, y = make_circles(n_samples=1000, noise=0.1, random_state=42)

# Underfit: linear model can't separate circular data
lr = LogisticRegression()
lr.fit(X_train, y_train)
print(f'Train: {lr.score(X_train, y_train):.4f}')  # ~0.5 (random!)

# Fix: use non-linear model or add polynomial features
from sklearn.svm import SVC
svm = SVC(kernel='rbf')  # RBF kernel handles non-linear boundary
svm.fit(X_train, y_train)
print(f'Train: {svm.score(X_train, y_train):.4f}')  # ~0.99
```

title: Data Leakage
definition: When information from outside the training data boundary (test/future data, or the target itself) is used during model training, leading to overly optimistic evaluation metrics that don't reflect real-world performance.
why_used: Data leakage is one of the most common and dangerous ML mistakes — models with leakage appear to perform brilliantly but fail catastrophically in production.
interview_point: Types of leakage: 1) Target leakage — a feature contains information that would only be available AFTER the prediction is needed (e.g., using 'days_in_hospital' to predict if someone gets hospitalized). 2) Train-test contamination — fitting a scaler/imputer on ALL data before splitting (test statistics contaminate training). Rule: ALWAYS fit preprocessing on TRAINING data ONLY.
limitations: Can make a worthless model appear 95%+ accurate in evaluation
use_cases: What to prevent — use sklearn Pipeline to avoid preprocessing leakage
real_world_example:
```python
import pandas as pd
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split

# WRONG: Leakage! Scaler sees test data statistics
scaler = StandardScaler()
X_all_scaled = scaler.fit_transform(X)  # LEAKAGE!
X_train, X_test = train_test_split(X_all_scaled)

# CORRECT: Fit scaler ONLY on training data
X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(X, y, test_size=0.2)
scaler = StandardScaler()
X_train_scaled = scaler.fit_transform(X_train)   # Fit on train only
X_test_scaled = scaler.transform(X_test)          # Apply to test

# BEST: Use Pipeline (handles this automatically)
from sklearn.pipeline import Pipeline
pipeline = Pipeline([('scaler', StandardScaler()), ('model', SVC())])
pipeline.fit(X_train, y_train)  # Scaler fit only on train fold in CV!
```

title: Class Imbalance
definition: When one class significantly outnumbers another in a classification dataset. A 99:1 ratio (e.g., fraud vs. legitimate transactions) causes models to be biased toward the majority class and fail to learn minority class patterns.
why_used: Class imbalance is the norm in real-world classification problems (fraud, medical diagnosis, anomaly detection). Understanding strategies to handle it is essential for any ML practitioner.
interview_point: Strategies in order of preference: 1) Change the metric (use F1, AUC, MCC instead of Accuracy). 2) class_weight='balanced' (built into sklearn). 3) SMOTE/oversampling. 4) Undersampling (risky — loses data). 5) Threshold tuning (adjust decision threshold from 0.5). 6) Ensemble methods (BalancedRandomForest).
advantages: N/A — imbalance is a challenge to overcome
limitations: Naive accuracy metric completely fails on imbalanced data
real_world_example:
```python
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestClassifier
from imblearn.over_sampling import SMOTE
from sklearn.metrics import classification_report

# Check imbalance
print(pd.Series(y_train).value_counts(normalize=True))

# Strategy 1: class_weight='balanced' (recommended first)
model = RandomForestClassifier(class_weight='balanced', n_jobs=-1)
model.fit(X_train, y_train)

# Strategy 2: SMOTE oversampling
smote = SMOTE(random_state=42)
X_resampled, y_resampled = smote.fit_resample(X_train, y_train)

# Strategy 3: Threshold tuning
y_prob = model.predict_proba(X_test)[:, 1]
threshold = 0.3  # Lower than 0.5 for better recall
y_pred = (y_prob >= threshold).astype(int)
print(classification_report(y_test, y_pred))
```
